policy_statement: Engage and expand nutrient management program participation by seeking feedback from historically unrepresented and beginning farmers and removing participation obstacles (e.g., focus groups, surveys, farm-to-farm education).
mechanism_description: Agencies seek feedback and use outreach (e.g., focus groups, surveys, and farm-to-farm education) to remove participation barriers so more farmers adopt nutrient management practices that reduce fertilizer-related emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Agricultural nutrient application practices → ↓ reactive nitrogen surplus in soils → ↓ nitrification/denitrification → ↓ N₂O emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: Agencies use outreach, feedback, and technical assistance to increase farmer enrollment and adoption of nutrient management (e.g., precision application, split applications, inhibitor use, manure nutrient accounting).  
(2) Process: Reduced over-application and better synchronization of nitrogen supply with crop uptake lowers soil mineral N available for microbial nitrification/denitrification that produces N₂O.  
(3) Outcome: Lower on-farm fertilizer-related N₂O emissions (a high-GWP greenhouse gas), reducing overall GHG emissions from agriculture.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced agricultural N₂O emissions by improving and scaling nutrient management practices that lower excess nitrogen in soils.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Increased adoption of nutrient management → reduced soil N₂O generation → reduced GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While nutrient management can also improve input efficiency and reduce water pollution, the mechanism as stated is explicitly aimed at fertilizer-related emissions reductions; the largest climate effect is direct GHG mitigation (N₂O).  
3) Edge cases applied: If implemented primarily for water quality or cost savings, it could resemble Resource Efficiency, but the canonical mechanism specifies emissions reduction as the causal endpoint; any co-benefits do not override the dominant GHG pathway.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy expanding nutrient management participation (via outreach/barrier removal) targets broader adoption of practices that reduce fertilizer-driven N₂O; the mitigation pathway remains primary across contexts.  
5) Note on secondary assignment: Potential secondary tags (e.g., Resource Efficiency, water-quality co-benefits) depend on policy-level evidence and are deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If “nutrient management” is implemented mainly as fertilizer-use reduction for cost savings without demonstrable N₂O benefits, or if it shifts emissions to other sources (e.g., increased ammonia leading to indirect N₂O), the net mitigation magnitude could vary; classification remains Mitigation because the canonical mechanism’s intended dominant effect is N₂O reduction.
key_indicators: "seek feedback"; "remove obstacles for participation"; "focus groups"; "surveys"; "farm-to-farm education"; "expand program participants"
co_benefits: Improved equity/inclusion in program access; farmer capacity-building and knowledge transfer; potential water quality improvements from better nutrient management.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be coded as an information/education intervention (information-disclosure) or an incentive-program support measure, but the text emphasizes engagement/barrier-removal strategy rather than payments or mandatory reporting.
